title: [Podman 3.2.3] Error: unable to start container ".....": error stat'ing file `/dev/input/event9`: No such file or directory: OCI runtime attempted to invoke a command that was not found
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

Also, there is a running list of known issues in the [Podman Troubleshooting Guide](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/master/troubleshooting.md),
please reference that page before opening a new issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

<!--
After successfully creating a container last night, I have attempted to enter it again today and have been presented with the following error when I run: `podman start cuda2004`:
`Error: unable to start container "cfc36b3a5ddc823de73a46a575020392cea2bbd7bcd5bdfcb68784763bf1197e": error stat'ing file `/dev/input/event9`: No such file or directory: OCI runtime attempted to invoke a command that was not found`
-->

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**
Unsure of the exact steps, but here are the installatio steps I took to create the container:

1. podman image pull nvcr.io/nvidia/cuda:11.4.1-devel-ubuntu20.04

2. 
```
podman run -d --hooks-dir /usr/share/containers/oci/hooks.d \
--name=cuda2004 --hostname=cuda2004 \
--volume /tmp/.X11-unix:/tmp/.X11-unix --env DISPLAY \
--device /dev/dri --device /dev/snd --device /dev/input \
--volume /etc/localtime:/etc/localtime:ro \
--volume /home/<user-name>/workspaces/podman:/mnt/podman:Z \
--restart=always --tmpfs /tmp --init --network=host \
nvcr.io/nvidia/cuda:11.4.1-devel-ubuntu20.04 tail -f /dev/null
```

3. Entering container as root: `podman exec --interactive --tty cuda2004 /bin/bash --login`
4. Entering container as created dev user: `podman exec --interactive --tty --user dev --workdir /home/dev/ cuda2004 /bin/bash --login`

**Describe the results you received:**
Container was created successfully and confirmed to work. Once I (what I assume) restarted my system, I was no longer able to enter the container.

**Describe the results you expected:**
To have the container ready on hand to enter and exit when needed without having to recreate it constantly.

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
podman version 3.2.3
```

**Output of `podman info --debug`:**


**Have you tested with the latest version of Podman and have you checked the Podman Troubleshooting Guide? (https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/master/troubleshooting.md)**


Yes

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**

comments.body:
Thanks for reaching out!

The container was created with `--device /dev/input` and it seems that `/dev/input/event9` wasn't present anymore after the reboot.  I think that Podman behaves correctly.  @giuseppe @Luap99 can you confirm?

No worries, thanks for the quick reply! If that is the case, what can I do to avoid this happening again? Would I need to omit the `-- device /dev/input` arg, or something else? Also, I am defining 3 devices:
`-device /dev/dri --device /dev/snd --device /dev/input \`

and I assume the `/dev/snd` and `/dev/dri` devices could also be an issue?

Another observation I have just made - I was creating a seperate container for ubuntu18.04 and after creating that I decided I wanted to see what other errors my cuda2004 container was giving, however to my surprise the container was able to be started and entered without any issues. I used a similar `podman run` command with the `--device` arg taking the same `/dev/` instances.
I think that using mounting volatile devices can always be an issue if the expectation is that the container can be restarted after reboot.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.

> @giuseppe @vrothberg @jabr0ni Is this still an issue?

To the best of my knowledge, nothing has changed. @mheon WDYT?

Concur. We've discussed this more extensively around `--privileged` (and having it recreate the list of devices mounted every time the container is restarted); perhaps this needs to be reworked for any folder of devices requested. Future feature territory, regardless.
does it work if you use `-v /dev/:/dev:rslave --mount type=devpts,destination=/dev/pts` ?

>  (and having it recreate the list of devices mounted every time the container is restarted)

I believe the correct fix in a scenario where host devices can change is to use `-v /dev/:/dev:rslave --mount type=devpts,destination=/dev/pts`.

If we recreate the list of devices when we start the container we have two issues:

1 - they won't be propagated once the container runs
2 - the is a TOCTOU race condition for what Podman sees and what the OCI runtime can bind mount.
I think we can close this issue.
